content:
A friendly, complete guide to RFC 10008, the problem it fixes, the old hacks we lived with, and when you can actually start using it.

9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you have ever built a search endpoint with fifteen filters, you already know this pain. You wanted GET, because reading data should be safe and cacheable. But your query was too big and too complex to stuff into a URL. So you reached for POST, felt a little dirty about it, and moved on.
HTTP just fixed that. A new method called QUERY landed as RFC 10008 in June 2026. It gives you the safety and caching of GET, plus the request body of POST. This post walks through the whole story, from the problem to the day you can ship it.
1. What Was the Problem
HTTP has always had a clean split between reading and writing.
GET is for reading. It is safe (it does not change anything on the server) and idempotent (calling it once or ten times gives the same result). Every browser, cache, CDN, and proxy on the planet already knows how to handle it. The catch is that GET has no meaningful request body. Everything you want to ask for has to fit in the URL.
That works fine for GET /users?role=admin. It falls apart the moment your query grows up.
Real search is rarely one filter. It is a role, a status, a date range, a sorted list of tags, a full text match, pagination, and a chunk of nested JSON that describes exactly what the client wants back. Try to cram all of that into a URL and you hit real walls:

• Length limits. Browsers and servers cap URL length somewhere between roughly 2,000 and 8,000 characters. A rich query blows past that.

• Encoding bloat. Special characters, spaces, and nested structures balloon once URL encoded, so you hit the limit even faster.

• Ambiguous structure. There is no agreed way to express arrays or nested objects in a query string. Everyone invents their own filter[status][]=active dialect.

• Leaky logs. URLs get logged everywhere: access logs, proxies, browser history, analytics. Sensitive filter values end up in plain text in a dozen places.
So the honest problem is simple. We needed a safe, repeatable, cacheable read, but we needed to send a real body to describe it. HTTP had no method for that.
2. What We Were Doing Earlier
Because the right tool did not exist, everyone picked one of three workarounds. Each one trades away something you actually wanted.
Workaround A: GET with a body
Technically you can attach a body to a GET request. In practice, do not rely on it. The HTTP spec never gave GET bodies a defined meaning, so every piece of infrastructure in the path is free to do its own thing with it.
GET /orders HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Content-Type: application/json{ "status": "active", "createdAfter": "2026–01–01", "limit": 50 }
And in a browser it is even worse, because fetch does not let you send a body on GET at all:
// TypeError: Request with GET/HEAD method cannot have body
await fetch("/orders", {
method: "GET",
body: JSON.stringify({ status: "active" }),
});
Why you cannot trust it:
- The body may be silently dropped. Many proxies, CDNs, and servers strip the body off a GET before it reaches your handler, so your filters just vanish.
- The request may be rejected outright. Some clients and gateways return a 400 the moment they see a body on GET.
- Behavior depends on the network path. It might work in local testing and then break in production once a load balancer or CDN sits in the middle.
- Browsers block it entirely. fetch and XMLHttpRequest refuse a body on GET, so this is a non-starter for frontend code.
- Caches get confused. A cache keys on the URL and ignores the body, so two different GET bodies can collide on the same cached response.
You cannot build a reliable API on behavior that changes depending on who is in the middle.
Workaround B: POST for search
This is what most teams actually shipped.
POST /orders/search HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json{ "status": "active", "createdAfter": "2026-01-01", "limit": 50 }It works, and it carries any body you want. But POST means “process this, and it may change state.” So you quietly lose everything that made a read nice:

• Caches and CDNs will not cache a POST response by default, because POST is not safe.

• Automatic retries become risky. Middleware cannot assume the request is repeatable, so a network blip can turn into a double action in systems that are not careful.

• The semantics lie. Anyone reading your logs or your API sees a write where there was only a read.
Workaround C: Cram it into the URL anyway
The other option is to force every filter into the query string, invent your own syntax for arrays and nested objects, and hope you stay under the length limit.
GET /orders?status=active&role=admin&createdAfter=2026–01–01
&tags[]=urgent&tags[]=vip&tags[]=new
&sort[0][field]=total&sort[0][dir]=desc
&ids=ord_0001,ord_0002,ord_0003,ord_0004,ord_0005,ord_0006,…// Encoding a nested filter into the URL gets ugly fast
const params = new URLSearchParams({
status: "active",
filter: JSON.stringify({ price: { gte: 1000, lte: 999999 } }),
});
const res = await fetch(`/orders?${params}`);
// /orders?status=active&filter=%7B%22price%22%3A%7B%22gte%22%3A1000%2C%22lte%22%3A999999%7D%7D
Why passing everything in the URL hurts:

• Length limits. Browsers and servers cap URLs somewhere between roughly 2,000 and 8,000 characters. A big ids list or a rich filter blows straight past that, and the request is rejected or truncated.

• Encoding bloat. Quotes, spaces, braces, and commas all balloon once URL encoded ({ becomes %7B), so you hit the limit far sooner than the raw text suggests.

• No standard for structure. There is no agreed way to express arrays or nested objects. Everyone invents their own dialect (tags[]=, filter[price][gte]=, comma lists), and clients and servers disagree on how to parse it.

• Sensitive data leaks. URLs are logged everywhere: access logs, proxy logs, browser history, analytics, referrer headers. Filter values like emails or IDs end up sitting in plain text in a dozen places.

• Painful to read and debug. A 3,000 character URL full of %22 and %5B is almost impossible to eyeball or share.

• Caching backfires. Every tiny change in filter order or whitespace produces a different URL, so your cache hit rate drops even though the query is effectively the same.

None of these are wrong exactly. They are all compromises we made because HTTP did not offer the method we actually needed.
3. How QUERY Solves It
QUERY is the missing method. In one line: it is a request that carries a body like POST, but is safe and idempotent like GET.
QUERY /orders HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Content-Type: application/json
Accept: application/json{
"select": ["id", "email", "total"],
"where": { "status": "active", "createdAfter": "2026-01-01" },
"sort": [{ "field": "total", "dir": "desc" }],
"limit": 50
}Here is what each design decision buys you.
The body carries the query. No URL length limit, no encoding gymnastics, no homemade array syntax. Send JSON, GraphQL, SQL-like filters, whatever your API speaks. Nothing sensitive leaks into the URL or the access log.
It is safe. QUERY promises it will not change server state. That single promise lets caches, CDNs, and proxies treat the response the way they treat GET.
It is idempotent. Running the same QUERY ten times is the same as running it once. So clients and middleware can safely retry on a dropped connection without fear of side effects.
It is cacheable, with a twist. The response can be cached, but the cache key has to include the request body, not just the URL. Two QUERY requests to the same path with different bodies are different queries. This is more work for cache implementations than GET, which is why caching support will arrive a bit slower.
It closes the loop with two headers. A successful QUERY response can return a Content-Location header pointing at the results, and a Location header pointing at an equivalent GET resource. That means the server can hand you back a plain URL that reproduces this exact query later, so you can bookmark, share, or replay a heavy query without resending the whole body every time.
A couple of strictness rules keep it clean. A QUERY request must include a Content-Type header, servers reject unsupported media types with a 415, and content sniffing (guessing the type from the bytes) is not allowed. The client says what it is sending, explicitly.
4. Pros and Cons
Nothing is free. Here is the honest ledger.
The good:

• Correct semantics at last. A read looks like a read, all the way through your stack.

• No URL length or encoding limits on the query.

• Safe automatic retries, because the method is idempotent.

• Cacheable by proxies and CDNs, unlike POST.

• Replayable and shareable queries through the Location header, without resending the body.

• Sensitive filters stay out of URLs and logs.
The trade-offs:

• Young ecosystem. Standardized in 2026. Some proxies, firewalls, WAFs, and load balancers do not recognize the method yet and may block it.

• Harder caching. Caching on the request body is more complex than caching on a URL, so cache support lags.

• Browser friction. QUERY is not a CORS safelisted method, so browser JavaScript triggers a CORS preflight before the real request. It works, it is just an extra round trip to plan for.

• Not bookmarkable by itself. A raw QUERY request is not a URL you can paste into an address bar. You lean on that returned Location URL for sharing.

• Tooling gaps. Not every client library, API gateway, or dashboard speaks QUERY yet, so you may hit rough edges.
5. Does QUERY Replace Anything, or Is It Just Nice to Have
It does not replace GET, and it does not replace POST. It fills the gap between them.
Think of it as three lanes:

• GET stays the right choice for simple reads with small parameters, and for anything you want to be bookmarkable or shareable as a plain URL.

• POST stays the right choice for writes and for actions that change state or are not safe to repeat.

• QUERY is the new lane for reads that need a real body: big, complex, or sensitive queries that should still be safe, idempotent, and cacheable.
So it is more than nice to have, because it gives a whole category of requests correct semantics for the first time. But it is not a migration you rush. You do not go rewrite every GET. You reach for QUERY specifically when a read has outgrown the URL.
6. Real World Use Cases
QUERY shines anywhere a read is too big or too rich for a URL.

• Complex search and filtering. Product catalogs, log search, analytics dashboards, and admin tables with many filters, ranges, and sort rules.

• Consolidating endpoints. Instead of a wall of /search, /filter, and /report POST endpoints, expose one QUERY endpoint per resource and let the body describe the request.

• GraphQL style and query language APIs. Send a query document in the body and keep read semantics honest.

• Geospatial and map queries. Bounding boxes, polygons, and layered filters that never fit cleanly in a query string.

• Reports and saved queries. Run a heavy report, get back a Location URL, and share or schedule it without shipping the body around again.

• Privacy sensitive lookups. Keep identifiers and filter values in the body and out of URLs, browser history, and logs.
7. When Can You Use It, and Support Status
QUERY is real and standardized, but the ecosystem around it is still catching up. Here is the picture as of mid 2026.

• The spec: Published as RFC 10008 in June 2026, on the standards track. This part is done.

• Node.js: Has parsed the QUERY method natively since early 2024, ahead of the final RFC.

• API tooling: OpenAPI 3.2 documents QUERY. API clients are adding it, for example Kreya shipped support in version 1.20.

• Server frameworks: Rolling out gradually. Spring was close but had not shipped support as of July 2026, and others are following.

• Browsers: Still ev
